title: Custom Iterators 🔄
description: Build your own iteration methods that gracefully handle blocks.

slides.title: Yielding with Data 🤝
slides.content:
The `yield` keyword is powerful because you can actually pass arguments **through** it, directly into the block!

This is exactly how iterators like `.each` manage to give you variables like `|index|`.

slides.title: Passing Values
slides.content: Watch closely: We pass "Bob" into the `yield` keyword. It flows out of the method, gets caught by the block parameter `|name|`, and is printed!
slides.codeSnippet:
def greet_user
  yield("Bob")
end

greet_user do |name|
  puts "Welcome to the site, #{name}!"
end

quizData.question: If a method says `yield(25)`, what must the attached code block look like to catch that number?
quizData.explanation: Just like how we iterated arrays, block parameters must always be wrapped in pipes `| |`.

slides.title: Multiple Yields 🔁
slides.content:
Who said you can only yield once?

If you put `yield` inside a loop within your method, it will trigger the block multiple times! 

This is secretly how the `.times` method is built beneath the surface of Ruby.

slides.title: Building .times
slides.content: We just built a custom iterator method from scratch! The `while` loop forces it to yield to the block 3 times.
slides.codeSnippet:
def three_times
  counter = 0
  while counter < 3
    yield(counter)
    counter += 1
  end
end

three_times do |num|
  puts "Loop executing: #{num}"
end

quizData.question: At its core, what is a custom iterator?

options.text: A standard method with a loop inside of it that repeatedly calls the `yield` keyword.
An array that has infinite items.

quizData.explanation: Iterators are just methods manipulating loops to trigger your attached blocks over and over again.

slides.title: Checking for Blocks 🔍
slides.content:
If your method has a `yield` keyword, but the user forgets to attach a block, the method will crash! 

You can prevent this by running `yield if block_given?`. The method will only yield if it detects a block was actually provided.
